chametz.definition: Food made from one of the five grains (wheat, barley, oats, spelt, rye) that has come into contact with water and remained so for more than 18 minutes — at which point it begins to ferment and becomes chametz. Chametz is forbidden to eat or derive benefit from on Passover, and there is a mitzvah to remove it before the holiday.

seor.definition: Old dough that has fermented extensively — similar to a sourdough starter. It has a very strong leavening power, which is why the Torah forbids it on Passover as well. Se'or differs from chametz in that it causes other doughs to leaven.

hagala.definition: Kashering a utensil by immersing it in vigorously boiling water. This method is used when chametz was cooked in a pot or spoon and the flavor was absorbed through liquid. Before hagala, the utensil is thoroughly cleaned, left unused for 24 hours, and then immersed in boiling water.

libun-kal.definition: Kashering a utensil by heating it with fire or in an oven until a piece of straw placed on the other side would be singed. This is done for utensils that had dry chametz placed on them (without sauce), such as baking trays and frying pans.

libun-chamur.definition: Heating a utensil with very strong fire until it begins to emit sparks or turns red from the heat. This is done for utensils that had chametz placed directly on fire — such as skewers or grill grates.

iruy.definition: Pouring boiling water from a kettle or boiling pot (kli rishon) over the surface to be kashered. This is useful for surfaces such as countertops, sinks, or tables. Sometimes a heated stone (even melubenet) is also used to strengthen the kashering.

iruy-kli-rishon.term: Iruy from Kli Rishon (עירוי מכלי ראשון)
iruy-kli-rishon.definition: Pouring boiling water directly from a vessel that was on a heat source (kli rishon). This method is stronger than pouring from a kli sheni, and sometimes a heated stone is added to reach a higher heat intensity.

kli-rishon.definition: A vessel that was directly on a heat source — such as a pot on the stove or an electric kettle. A kli rishon is the vessel in which the food was cooked or the water was boiled, and it has a strong ability both to cook and to kasher.

kli-sheni.definition: A vessel into which contents were poured from the kli rishon — for example, a bowl into which boiling soup was poured. The cooking and kashering power of a kli sheni is weaker, and it is insufficient for most types of Passover kashering.

ben-yomo.definition: A utensil in which chametz was cooked within the last 24 hours. Such a utensil has absorbed a strong chametz flavor and may not be used for Passover before kashering. After 24 hours have passed, the absorbed flavor is considered spoiled, and there are certain leniencies in its kashering.

biur-chametz.definition: Destroying the chametz in one's possession — usually by burning, but also by scattering to the wind, throwing into the sea, or crumbling and discarding. This is done on the 14th of Nisan before the deadline for eating chametz. After the destruction, one nullifies any remaining chametz.
examples: Burning chametz on the morning of the 14th

matza-shmura.definition: Matza made from flour that has been guarded from the time of wheat harvest to ensure it does not come into contact with water, so it will not ferment. This is the most preferred matza, and some are careful to eat only shmura matza throughout all of Passover. On the Seder night, there is a Torah obligation to eat specifically shmura matza.
examples: Shmura matza from field to baking

matza-ashira.definition: Matza kneaded with fruit juice, wine, honey, or eggs — without water. According to most halachic authorities, it is not considered matza for the purpose of fulfilling the mitzvah of eating matza on the Seder night, so one cannot fulfill the obligation with it. Some have the custom not to eat it at all during Passover, out of concern that it may have fermented.

kitniyot.definition:
Types of seeds such as rice, corn, lentils, chickpeas, and beans. Although kitniyot are not chametz and do not ferment — Ashkenazi Jews adopted the custom of not eating them on Passover.
The custom spread mainly in medieval Europe for several reasons:
(1) Flours can be made from kitniyot, which might be confused with grain flour.
(2) Grain and kitniyot fields were sometimes mixed, raising concern of cross-contamination.
(3) When cooked, kitniyot swell and appear to "rise" like leavened dough.
Therefore, the custom to prohibit eating them was established, even though they are technically permitted by law.
In contrast, most Sephardic Jews did not adopt this custom — and permit kitniyot on Passover, as long as there is no concern of chametz contamination.

even-melubenet.definition: A stone (or piece of iron) heated until it is white-hot, used to strengthen the kashering process — mainly when boiling water is poured over it, to ensure the heat is sufficiently high. This is required according to some halachic authorities for countertops or sinks where chametz was absorbed through direct heat.

kebolao-kach-polto.definition: A fundamental principle of kashering utensils: the way a utensil absorbed the forbidden flavor is the way it must expel it. For example, if chametz was absorbed through cooking in water — it is kashered by hagala (boiling). If absorbed through fire — it is kashered by libun (burning). Each utensil according to its use.
examples: Pot used for cooking needs hagala

taarovet-chametz.definition: A food or product that contains chametz mixed with other ingredients — even in a very small amount. For example: a sauce or cookie containing flour, oats, or beer. A chametz mixture is forbidden on Passover even if the flavor is barely noticeable, and one may not keep it in one's possession.

countertop.process: Clean thoroughly and pour boiling water (directly from the boiling vessel). Alternatively, cover the entire countertop with heavy aluminum foil
chumrot: Pour boiling water and also cover with heavy aluminum foil
countertop.notes: A delicate countertop on which hot items were never placed — cleaning and pouring only

sink.process: Clean thoroughly and pour boiling water (directly from the boiling vessel)
chumrot: It is proper to pour boiling water three times
In addition to pouring, place a plastic basin inside the sink or cover with heavy aluminum foil
sink.notes: Dry the sink first so the boiling water touches the sink directly

stovetop.process: Clean thoroughly and kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon, or alternatively cover with heavy aluminum foil
chumrot: The custom is to turn on all burners for about half an hour
stovetop.notes: You can also kasher the stovetop by pouring boiling water from a kli rishon over it. Other metal parts that don't touch pots (enamel surface, gas heads, burners) just need thorough cleaning

electric-stovetop.process: Clean thoroughly and heat on the highest setting for half an hour

oven.process: Clean thoroughly and run on the highest heat for half an hour to an hour
chumrot: It is preferable to not use the oven for 24 hours before kashering
Some are strict not to kasher a baking oven for Pesach
oven.notes: When kashering the oven, you can leave the rack in, then place aluminum foil on the rack and use disposable trays on top of it. A self-cleaning oven (~500°C) does not need cleaning as it is considered libun chamur.

baking-trays.process: Cannot be used on Pesach as they require libun chamur which would damage them. Those who are lenient to kasher them by hagala have what to rely on
baking-trays.notes: When kashering the oven, you can leave the rack, then place aluminum foil on the rack and use disposable trays on top

hot-plate.process: Clean thoroughly and pour boiling water from a kli rishon, or alternatively wrap in aluminum foil
chumrot: After cleaning, heat for an hour then wrap in aluminum foil

microwave.process: Clean thoroughly of food residue, wait 24 hours without use, then place a bowl of water and heat the microwave for three minutes
chumrot: Rabbi Mordechai Eliyahu ruled that in addition, one must cover all foods being heated
microwave.notes: When heating food on Pesach, place something to separate between the rotating plate and the food being heated

dishwasher.process: Clean thoroughly of food residue (especially the filter), and run the longest program on the highest heat with detergent
chumrot: Ideally, kasher the dishwasher racks by immersing in boiling water or pouring boiling water over them, or replace them
dishwasher.notes: You can leave the racks inside the dishwasher and they will be kashered as well

knife.process: Kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
chumrot: Use special cutlery for Pesach

fork.process: Kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
chumrot: Use special cutlery for Pesach

spoon.process: Kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
chumrot: Use special cutlery for Pesach

table.process: Clean thoroughly and spread a new, clean tablecloth
chumrot: Some are strict to pour boiling water on the tables if they are sturdy and won't be damaged
table.notes: A table on which hot chametz food was never placed, or was always covered with a tablecloth or glass, only needs thorough cleaning. On Pesach, it's good to avoid placing hot pots directly on the table

tablecloth.process: Wash in a washing machine, and that kashers them for Pesach
chumrot: Some are strict to use a new tablecloth for Pesach

refrigerator.process: Clean thoroughly and ensure there are no food residues at all
refrigerator.notes: In places that are hard or impossible to clean, you can pour soapy water or a substance that spoils food, and that is sufficient

kitchen-cabinets.process: Clean thoroughly and ensure there are no food residues at all
kitchen-cabinets.notes: In places that are hard or impossible to clean, you can pour soapy water or a substance that spoils food, and that is sufficient

ceramic.process: If they came in contact with hot food, they cannot be kashered at all and must be stored away until after Pesach
ceramic.notes: If used only with cold food, they can be kashered by rinsing. But if chametz liquid (e.g., beer) was in them for 24 hours, they can be kashered by soaking for three days (see glossary)

glass.process: Rinse thoroughly and it can be used on Pesach — even if it touched boiling chametz, no hagala is needed
chumrot: Some are strict with glass and don't use it on Pesach, treating it like metal utensils (Ben Ish Chai Year 1, Tzav 14)

plastic.process: If they came in contact with hot chametz food, kasher them by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon

enamel.process: Can be kashered by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
chumrot: Some recommend kashering three times (Rabbi Ovadia Yosef, Rabbi Mordechai Eliyahu)

pots.process: Clean thoroughly and kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon, including handles and lids
pots.notes: Pay attention to rinse around the handles so no residue remains, and clean rust as much as possible

mortar.process: Clean thoroughly and kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
mortar.notes: If used only with cold food and not with sharp items, no hagala is needed — just thorough cleaning

pan.process: Clean thoroughly and kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
pan.notes: Note that if it's a Teflon pan, see the entry for "Teflon Pan"

teflon-pan.process: If it was used most of the year with oil, it can be kashered by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
chumrot: Teflon pans are not kashered because they are commonly used without oil and would then require libun chamur

toaster.process: Requires libun chamur, and since it may get damaged — it cannot be kashered

dentures.process: Clean thoroughly and it is kashered for Pesach — no hagala needed
chumrot: It is good to pour boiling water from a kli rishon over them

skewers.process: Require libun by fire until sparks fly off them
skewers.notes: If it is unknown whether chametz was mixed in them, they can be kashered by hagala in a kli rishon

wood.process: Thorough cleaning and hagala by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
wood.notes: If used in a different way, e.g. in a kli sheni etc., kasher according to its use

stone.process: Thorough cleaning and hagala by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
stone.notes: If used in a different way, e.g. in a kli sheni etc., kasher according to its use

metal.process: Thorough cleaning and hagala by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
metal.notes: If used in a different way, e.g. in a kli sheni etc., kasher according to its use

bone.process: Thorough cleaning and hagala by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
bone.notes: If used in a different way, e.g. in a kli sheni etc., kasher according to its use

meat-grinder.process: After disassembling and thorough cleaning, in holes and cracks, kasher by immersing in boiling water in a kli rishon
meat-grinder.notes: It is proper to burn the parts with holes and cracks by fire to burn every crumb

thermos.process: Thorough cleaning and pouring boiling water from a kli rishon. If used only for water, cleaning alone is sufficient

chumrot: A new toothbrush for Pesach

water-urn.process: Clean thoroughly and that's it. But if chametz was heated on it (bourekas/bread), then kasher the lid
water-urn.notes: If chametz touched the body of the urn while it was boiling, do not use the urn as it cannot be kashered

books.notes: Similarly, small benchers and grace after meals booklets — if clean from residue, they can be used on Pesach

pan.process: Libun kal (until straw would burn on the other side)
chumrot: Some practice libun chamur

glass-cup.process: Hagala three times in boiling water

knife.process: Hagala in boiling water three times, including the handle
chumrot: Some have the custom to buy special cutlery for Pesach

countertop.process: Thorough cleaning, pouring boiling water, covering with aluminum foil or another surface

ceramic.notes: Must buy new utensils or use utensils designated for Pesach

oven.process: Thorough cleaning, wait 24 hours, run at maximum heat for one hour
chumrot: Many have the custom not to use the oven for Pesach but to cover it with aluminum foil

microwave.process: Thorough cleaning, boiling a cup of water with soap for 10 minutes
chumrot: Some have the custom to use only with a double cover

knife.process: Hagala in boiling water three times, including the handle
chumrot: Some have the custom to buy special cutlery for Pesach

ceramic.notes: Must buy new utensils or use utensils designated for Pesach

countertop.process: Thorough cleaning. The custom is to pour boiling water from a kli rishon on areas where hot items are placed.
chumrot: Pouring with a heated stone

sink.process: Thorough cleaning. Pour boiling water from a kli rishon.
sink.notes: If hot chametz was cooked or poured into it — be especially careful with the pouring.

stovetop.process: Thorough cleaning and lighting the burners to kasher the grates.

electric-stovetop.process: Thorough cleaning and running on highest heat for about half an hour.

oven.process: Thorough cleaning and running on highest heat for at least half an hour.

baking-trays.process: Cannot be used. Use disposable trays.
baking-trays.notes: You can kasher the rack and place aluminum foil and a disposable tray on it.

glass-cup.process: Thorough cleaning. No additional kashering needed.

pots.process: Hagala in a kli rishon after thorough cleaning.
pots.notes: If used on fire without liquid — libun is required.

pan.process: Libun kal, since they are usually used with little oil.
pan.notes: If used on direct fire without oil — libun chamur.

cutlery.process: Hagala in a kli rishon after thorough cleaning.

dishwasher.process: Thorough cleaning, run the dishwasher with detergent on high heat without dishes.

ceramic.process: Cannot be kashered. Cannot be used on Pesach if they were used with hot chametz.
ceramic.notes: Ideally, use utensils designated for Pesach.

metadata.title: Kashering Utensils for Passover
metadata.description: A clear and precise halachic guide to kashering utensils for Passover: hagala, libun, iruy, chametz and se'or - including sources from the Torah, Gemara and Poskim. Suitable for all levels.
metadata.keywords: kashering utensils, Passover, Pesach, chametz, hagala, libun, se'or, iruy, Passover laws, kitchen kashering

about.title: About
about.subtitle: A guide to kashering utensils for Passover
about.missionContent: This website was created to help Jews around the world prepare their kitchens for Passover. We provide clear and accurate halachic information about kashering utensils, based on sources from the Torah, Gemara, and Poskim.
about.feature1: Easy and quick search for kitchen utensils and their kashering methods
about.feature2: Comprehensive glossary to understand halachic terms
about.feature3: Detailed halachic sources for every instruction
about.disclaimerContent: The information on this site is for general education only and does not replace halachic rulings. For halachic questions, please consult a Rabbi.

home.title: Kashering Utensils for Passover
home.subtitle: Search for a utensil and discover how to kasher it
home.searchPlaceholder: Search for a utensil (e.g., pot, pan, cup)

glossary.title: Passover Glossary
glossary.subtitle: All the important terms for understanding kashering utensils for Passover

contact.title: Contact Us
contact.dialogDescription: Who can discern errors? Cleanse me of hidden faults
contact.description: We'd love to hear from you! For questions, comments and suggestions:
contact.personalMessage: I'd be happy to hear from you about any suggestions for improvement, bug reports, etc. - Emmanuel.

footer.disclaimer: The information on this site is for general education only. For halachic questions, please consult a Rabbi.
